title: When to Call Hospice, the Doctor, or 911 After a Death
summary: Knowing which call to make first removes one layer of confusion during an already disorienting moment.
category: Death of a Loved One (Transition into After-a-Loss)
journeyStage: Death of a Loved One (Transition into After-a-Loss)
author: HarborCare Circle Editorial
seoTitle: When to Call Hospice, the Doctor, or 911 After a Death | HarborCare Circle
metaDescription: Knowing which call to make first removes one layer of confusion during an already disorienting moment.
whatYouNeedToKnow: Expected death under a doctor's care (not hospice): call the doctor's office.

paragraphs: If your loved one was enrolled in hospice, the hospice team is the correct first call — they will send a nurse to confirm the death and guide next steps, and they do this specifically so families don't need to involve emergency services.

If the death was expected but hospice wasn't involved, the treating doctor's office can guide you, including whether to call the funeral home directly. If death was sudden, unwitnessed, or unexpected, calling 911 is the appropriate step, even if it feels like the situation is already resolved.

nextSteps: Save the hospice or doctor's after-hours number somewhere accessible in advance.
Know which category applies before a crisis, if possible.
Call 911 without hesitation for any unexpected death.
disclaimer: This is general guidance — if you are ever unsure which call to make, calling 911 is always an acceptable choice.

sources.name: National Hospice and Palliative Care Organization
